sentences.text: Well, you know, life was so tranquil, seemingly so tranquil, at onset of the fall of 1939.
I remember August, '39 was a little bit unsettling.
We really didn't know what is going to hit us, but something was ominous in the air.

The kind of life that we have had-- and I did not mention previously my mother, and I need to give her some mention, simply because she was a very big force, a very strong pillar in our family structure.
She was a very wise woman.
She enjoyed tremendous reputation in this entire town as a woman who could give advice to anyone who needed advice.
And she was a very charitable person, and she really was the pillar of our family.
And I remember, it was August.
And my older sister went away on vacation for the first time.
My mother allowed her to go on vacation by herself with some friends.
And she went to the mountains to the south of Poland.
And rumors had it that something is going to happen.
Of course, politically there was a meeting between Molotov and Ribbentrop, and this was the nonaggression pact between Germany and Russia.
And people were wondering why?
Why did they enter into this kind of a pact?
And then there was mobilization in Poland.
People, all people of any military rank, were mobilized.
Nobody knew why.
So there was that unsettling feeling.
And my mother said, you know, we have to get Fela back home, because I don't know what's going to happen.
And I want her home.
And sure enough, we wired her.
And I remember she responded, and she came home.
And that was late August.
And all of a sudden, we heard that there were some sightings of airplanes in the air somewhere.
Not in Radomsko, but somewhere.
And we decided that perhaps we ought to ward ourselves against bombs.
So what we did, we taped window panes in our, in our house, in our home.
And everybody tried to get the tape and tape the window panes.
And that was our security against the bombs, should they fall.
Well, sure enough, we went to sleep the last day of August.
And very early that morning, we heard a noise.
And that was early, about 6 o'clock in the morning, on September 1st.
The first bomb fell.
German bombs fell on our town, on Radomsko.
And it was outside, on the outskirts of the town.
And something with smoldering.
We saw some smoke coming out from somewhere into the sky.
And rumors had it that the war broke out.
Of course, there was already no way to communicate with anybody.
There were no newspapers, and there was-- radio did not know themselves.
The announcers didn't know themselves what was happening.
So we immediately decided that we are not going to stay in town.
My father went down to the store and took whatever cash he had there.
And we came upstairs and my mother said to us, take suitcase.
Each of you take a suitcase, and take all your personal things in the suitcase.
Whatever you need, take it with you because we are going to leave.
And they packed big, big valises, one with the silver and one with furs.
And they decided to bring these two valises to a very good friend of my father's-- of course, a Pole-- and left it for safekeeping.
But nevertheless, we took a lot of things with us.
And my mother said, you know, I have a cousin to the east of Radomsko in a town named Konskie.
And we looked for anything.
Some kind of a vehicle to take us east of Radomsko.
And sure enough, a neighbor of ours found a pickup truck.
And we shared the pickup truck with him, and we went to Konskie.
We arrived in Konskie Friday night, which was Shabbat, which was a no no.
Which was something that nobody would do.
And of course, when we arrived there, they just were overwhelmed.
They couldn't understand how we would have dared to come to their home, into the Shabbat already.
Well, we explained to them that the Germans are really coming, and they were incredulous.
They really didn't believe us.
Well, we stayed through the Shabbat.
And sure enough, two days after we got there, the Germans got there.
And this was one of the very first horrors that we experienced, because the Germans went to all the homes of the father-- most prominent Jewish people.
They came to my mother's cousin's-- his name was Rafalowicz-- to his house.
He had four sons.
And they took all the males out of the house, including my father.
And they took them to the outskirts of town, in the morning.
And we didn't know what was happening with all these people.
And we were waiting for their return.
And sure enough, they came back at the end of the day, ashen gray.
My father was there, and the boys were there.
But the boys and my father had to dig a trench after they had killed the father.
And of course, that was the shock of our lives.
This was-- to us, it was just unbelievable.
So we stayed a few days, through this shiva, and then we returned home.
When we returned back to Radomsko, we found out that our house had been totally bombed.
There was just rubble.
We couldn't find anything.
I remember we looked-- went to the side, when we found pieces of dishes, and the string of the piano, and such things.
Pieces of wallpaper.

And here we were, my mother, my father, and the three of us, who have lived in great comfort, without a home, without a pillow, without a blanket, without anything to our names except those few personal suitcases.
Well, somebody took pity on us.
And he was a young man-- he recently married-- who knew us very, very well and invited us to share his apartment.
And it was a railroad apartment, and he gave us one room.
And I remember coming to that apartment, and they very graciously said, this will be your room.
And we realized that we will have to share a bedroom with my mother and father, all five of us.
And share the kitchen.
And this was a traumatic experience to my parents.
Very traumatic experience.
This was--
This was--
This was a Jewish family, obviously.
And that was 1939.
That was the beginning of the war, and we were already homeless.
And we were without any means of income.
We had-- we were totally left without any means for sustenance.
And there was the question, how in the world are we going to live?
Well, the Germans took care of that, because soon after, they created a ghetto.
And you couldn't exist in the ghetto unless you registered in order to get ration cards.
There were no place to go and buy food.
There was no place to earn a living.
Once the ghetto was established, we really couldn't go outside of the ghetto.
They, of course, established a system of identification.
And where we lived, we had to wear a white band with a blue star of David.
And that was our identity.
And you could not, absolutely, be found without it, in the ghetto or outside of the ghetto.
Certainly outside of to ghetto, it would have meant death to you, because once you were identified as a Jew, you had absolutely no way of existing outside of the ghetto, unless you were in hiding.
Well, this was Radomsko.
It was our town of Radomsko.
And the very important part of our ghetto was that very early on, they would bring people, Jewish people, from villages surrounding our town into our ghetto.
And we had a very large synagogue.
And they would drop them off at the synagogue.
And you know, in the orthodox synagogues, there is an upstairs and a downstairs.
An upstairs for the women, downstairs for the men.
Well, we would bring all the families upstairs, and we would divide the spaces.
And we would divide them by hanging a sheet to give them some privacy.
So that the family unit would have a little bit of a space, divided by a sheet.
And we would arrange a committee so that every, every family residing in the ghetto would share whatever they had in food with a family that was brought in from other ghettos.
And that was the beginning of the misery.
You mean the Polish people?
Well, the Polish people were, unfortunately-- and I must say that because that is the truth-- the Polish people were not, not kindly disposed towards Jews.
They were, they were really anti-Semitic, simply because it was ingrained in them.
The truth of the matter is, that unfortunately, it was the Christian religion that really did that indoctrination.
Those of us who know the Gospels would know that if you teach the gospel, if you read John or Matthew, you just straight understand.
You-- there is a straight line.
Jews are not of God the Father.
They are of their father, the devil.
And these things were [? encalculated ?] into them.
And of course, this was simply a brainwashing system of preaching to the Jews, that Jews were responsible for the deicide, for the murder of Christ.
And they held it over our heads all the time.
Well, I must say that we, we, we encountered some very good exceptions.

One exception was a judge in town who was a good friend of my father's, and who, eventually, really, is responsible for saving all three of us after he had found out that my mother and father were taken away.
There was also one woman who was very fond of my mother, a Christian, who tried to help me out very, very much, and who would come to the ghetto, and who would bring me some food occasionally.
But the majority of the Polish population, who were very, very Catholic and Christian, would not be very, very kindly disposed.

And we had one proof of it when we came back after we would liberated to that gentlemen where-- to whom my parents entrusted the silver and the fur who denied ever getting it, and who wouldn't give it back to us.
So the ghetto was a very, very restrictive, and a very restricting area, where we really had a very, a very limited space.
Not only the actual living space, but actually, also, the limited outside space.
And that ghetto was keeping to-- keeping us very much together in, in, in a way that it brought us together as well.
We would try to establish semblance of a social life, semblance of an intellectual life.
For example, there were no schools.
And my younger sister was very young, and I knew that she needed education.
So I established a clandestine school.
And the girls would come to my room.
I can't call it an apartment.
And they would be in groups of five or sixes, and they would come every hour on the hour.
And we would-- I would conduct classes with them.
And I would spill out of my head whatever I remembered having learned in my school.
And the most incongruous subject matters.
I would teach them Latin vocabulary.
I would teach them geography.
I would, I would teach them anything I knew.

As a matter of fact, I wrote an essay, which I call 'Gone with a Dream' because one of the things that I read most recently, when-- prior to establishing the school, was the Polish translation of And when the girls came-- and they were so solemn, and they were so sorrowful.
And they would say, please, please tell us a story.
I would say, OK.
Today I'm going to tell you to a-- take you to an altogether different world.
And I took them away to a southern plantation in Georgia.
And I was talking to them about Tara, and about Melanie, and about Scarlet and, and Brett Butler.
And their eyes would open, and they would dream about it.
And I remember one girl, especially, green-eyed girl who, who, who would-- I would have thought would have looked just like Scarlett.
I said, goodbye Scarlett.
And she would say, no, I'd rather be Melanie.
So we were dreaming together.
Our ghetto did not have a wall, but our ghetto was guarded.
And in certain places, we had fences, fences that were, that were strategically placed.
But it was-- most of it was an open ghetto.
In other words, without the wall in the ghetto, in the very beginning.
Then, of course, the guards were there constantly.
I mean, we knew that we couldn't leave the ghetto because the guards were there.
And there were different guards at different times.
Most of the guards were not German.
Most of the guards were Ukrainian or Poles.
And somebody once mentioned that there were also some Lithuanians there, although I never saw a Lithuanian.
But I did meet Ukrainians and Poles.
And also, the Jewish militia was also forced into guarding the ghetto on the outside.
There was a whole administration in the ghetto, so-called Judenrat.
And Judenrat was a, a board of, of Jews who were entrusted with some kind of an administrative leadership.
Unfortunately, that leadership had a double-- was a double-edged sword, because they had to execute all the proclamations by, by the Germans.
And the proclamations came fast and furious.
At first, they would tell us to surrender all our valuables, all our jewelry.
Then they would tell us to surrender all our furs.
Then we had to surrender blankets.
Then they would come and ask us to surrender copper and nickel artifacts, anything that they could, could transfer into other implements of war.
And they didn't just rest at the proclamations.
They would have raids.
And if they weren't satisfied at the number of items that they would collect, they would come, sometimes-- mostly-- in the middle of the night.
And they would raid the apartments, and they would turn it upside down.
And they would take whatever they could lay their hands on.
So those raids, at first, were for collecting things.
But then they were doing raids for collecting people.
As a young girl, how did your mind comprehend what was going on?
I really cannot answer how my mind comprehended it.
I think that in situations, in emergency situations, what you do, you work on your instinct.
You do not have enough time, or the luxury, to philosophize and try to analyze as to what does this mean to you-- to me.
What we were worrying about is how to survive as a family unit, how to protect each other, and how to make sure that we don't lose dignity.
And the unfortunate thing is that this was the first thing we had to lose, is the dignity.
Because I remember one time when they came into the house, to the apartment, and they took my father and they started kicking him.
And my father looked at us and cried.
And this is the first time I saw him crying, because he, he, he saw his family looking at him, being kicked by a boot of an SS man.
And this reminds me of a story that Zvi Kolitz wrote.
It's entitled Yossel Rakover, who was an Orthodox Jew, is being mistreated by the Gestapo.
He had just lost his wife and his children, murdered in his presence.
And there is a Gestapo man facing him with a dog right there at his heel.
And he realizes that the Gestapo man treats the dog better than he is being treated.
And he says, I was ashamed, in front of the dog, to be a man.
And I felt the same way about my father.
He was ashamed in front of his children, how he was treated.
So we really were totally, totally committed to the proposition that we don't know how to deal with it, but we must deal with it.
Because he may have been slow in exiting.
He was beaten because the SS didn't like the way he was moving about.
I mean, this happened to me too.
Once they made a raid, and they wanted all the young women to come out and to scrub the floor in the mess hall with a toothbrush.
A really small, small brushes, the size of a toothbrush.
And if we didn't work fast enough, they will hit us in the butt with a-- with a boot.
We would be kicked in the boot as we were on our hands and knees.
And, and, you, you, you couldn't react.
I had a friend who, who was mistreated by an SS man.
She went berserk.
She went absolutely berserk, and she actually lost her mind.
And he was-- she was shot on the spot because she ran into the SS man, totally bewildered.
And she was shot right then and there.
So we had to keep our wits around us, about us.
We had to know that we cannot do anything about it except to survive, and to hope for the best.
Did you have sufficient food while you were still in the first ghetto?
Well, the food was rationed, and the food was not sufficient.
But what we did was, fortunately, my mother had the presence of mind to take as much of her jewelry with her as possible.
And it was not uncommon to exchange a diamond earring for a pound of butter.
It was not uncommon to take a, a beautiful brooch and exchange it for whatever was offered at the, at the gate in bartering.
You couldn't buy anything.
You would barter.
And you would barter anything and everything.
First you would barter the jewelry.
Once you've exhausted the jewelry, you would barter your clothing.
You would barter whatever you had.
And then of course-- and I remember it very well.

You would, quite often, at the very end of the ghetto, I would go into garbage pails and I would look for scraps of, of potato peels, and wash them, and clean them, and put them in the pot with a little salt, and call it soup.
So we went through all kinds of stages, as far as provisions were concerned.
No one worried about kosher and non kosher any longer.
No one worried whether we mixed the, the, the meat with the, the milk any longer.
We have to look away.
We had to be very, very pragmatic.
And the pragmatism of the whole thing was survival.
And the conditions for-- the sanitation conditions, were you able to bathe?
Were you able to have toilets?
Well, bathing was really not considered necessary any longer.
We used to wash.
Skimpily, we used to wash.
Some apartments had toilets.
Some apartments had outhouses.
But you asked the first question, I remember what it was about--
Sanitation.
The sanitation was very poor.
And because sanitation was very poor, there was an epidemic of typhus, or typhoid.
And because we had only one doctor in the ghetto and we had no hospitals, we had a very, very high death rate.
And many people died like flies, because they were telling us that the typhoid was carried by lice.
And we couldn't really deal with lice.
Everybody had lice in their hair.
And they were saying that if you put turpentine on you hair, you can kill the lice.
And it was just not doable.
It was just not doable.
We really didn't have the wherewithal to deal with an epidemic.
I-- I'll tell you an incident that I write about.
I developed very sharp pains in my abdomen, and the one doctor we had diagnosed it as very acute appendicitis.
And I needed to have surgery.
And my father, through some connections, connected with it friend of his, a Polish policeman, who took a note from my father to a surgeon in town.
And he begged him to operate on him.
They took me out in a, in a wagon which was used to taking corpses to the cemetery.
And they covered me with straw and rags.
And they took me at night out to a back entrance to the hospital.
And the surgeon met me there.
And single handedly, without any help-- no nurses, nobody-- he got there and he administered the anesthesia, local anesthesia.
And he administered-- and he did the operation.
And he put me to recover in a closet.
They put a small bed in the closet, and I was there for, I think, a day and a half or two days.
And then I was brought home the same way.
And of course, my parents never knew whether or not I survived the operation until I was brought back.
So the, the situation was very, very acute when it came to sanitation.
Health problems-- people who were sick, they were just sick.
You couldn't afford the luxury of being sick.
And my case of that operation was very unusual, and very fortuitous.
And it was really only thanks to my father's reputation in town that he was able to, to pull it off.
In--
In the ghetto?
Yeah.
I mean-- yes.
In the ghetto.
I'm sorry.
Well, you know what actions are and what selections are?
There were a number of actions, number of selections, where they put-- took people away to so-called labor camps.
One of the most horrible-- we called them Bloody Tuesday, I think it was Tuesday.
October-- October-- between October 8th or 10th of 1942, they had the most horrible, more horrible action.
We were just sitting around.
And just-- we moved about.
This was our, our third place.
Third ghetto.
That was a very restricted, small ghetto.
And we were just in the room and our parents were elsewhere.
They went to the next building, and all of the sudden, we saw the SS men in the courtyard with the dogs running into our building and looking for people.
And we knew that there was an attic in the building that we had prepared.
My parents actually were instrumental in it.
Such an ironical twist of events.
So my sisters and I ran immediately-- went immediately into the attic.
And they would already a number of people there.
And we were the last to come in.
And then we-- they closed up the attic with some bricks.
And we knew we were separated from our parents, and we didn't know what happened to them.
And we were in that attic several days.
And we heard the dogs going up and down.
And we heard the shouts and the footsteps of the Gestapo people, the SS men.
And here we are in the attic, that was about two feet high.
Everybody lying, couldn't sit up.
And all we had there-- we were totally unprepared-- was one bucket for, for emergency.
And there was a big, tall bottle of cherries.
The ones that you made [? vishnak ?] from.
And I remember, I put myself in charge of the cherries.
And I would dish out one cherry per meal, per person, to sustain us.
